sections.title: The big contrast — finished vs connected to now
sections.content:
**Past simple** = a **finished time**, separate from now. **Present perfect** = **connected to now** — the experience or result still matters.
- *I lived in Prague in 2020.* (over, separate from now)
- *I have lived in Prague.* (the experience is part of who I am today)
The question is never "which event happened?" but "how does the speaker frame it?" — the heart of the Verb Phrase root (#2 in the Tree Model): the perfect is English's main marker of **result and present relevance**.

items.good: I worked at Skanska for three years.
items.why: finished — I don't work there now

items.good: I have worked at Skanska for three years.

diagrams.text: Czech has no direct equivalent: *byl jsem v Praze* covers both *I was in Prague* (finished) and *I have been to Prague* (experience). English forces the choice — the single biggest tense-confusion fix for Czech learners, in job interviews, exams, CVs and everyday speech.

sections.title: At a glance
sections.content: The whole contrast on one card.

diagrams.title: Past simple vs present perfect — at a glance
rows: *I have gone, she has finished, they have seen*
ever, never, just, already, yet, since, for
*I have seen him* (at some point — relevant now).*

sections.title: Time markers — the decisive clue
sections.content:
In about 90% of exam gap-fills (and most real speech), the **time marker** locks the tense — the fastest, most reliable rule in English grammar.
*Yesterday* forbids present perfect; *since 2020* forbids past simple.

diagrams.title: Time markers — past simple vs present perfect
rows: **so far, up to now, recently, lately**

sections.title: Which tense?
sections.content: Ask in order — the first *yes* decides.

steps.q: A finished-time marker — yesterday, ago, last…, in 2020, when I was a child?
since / for — started in the past and still true now?
Life experience, no specific time (ever / never)?
News — the result still matters now?

steps.end: Finished, separate from now → past simple

sections.title: News and recent results — the conversation shift
sections.content:
**Present perfect breaks news. Past simple gives details.** Listen for the switch — it happens constantly in real conversation.
The pattern is everywhere — work updates, accidents, achievements, gossip: announce first (*She's got the job! / The boss has just left / I've finished the report*), then details slide into past simple. Mixing them up sounds odd and slows the listener down.

items.why: present perfect — the news, the result is now

items.good: B: Oh no — how did it happen?
items.why: past simple — asking about the finished moment

items.good: A: I fell off my bike yesterday.
I've lost my wallet. → I lost it on the metro this morning.
items.why: news (I don't have it now) → details, finished time

items.good: She has won the contract! → She signed it yesterday.
items.why: news, result now → specific past moment

sections.title: Life experience vs specific past — the classic trap
sections.content:
Someone's **life so far**, no specific time in mind → **present perfect**. Mention **a particular time** → switch to **past simple**.
The #1 trap in speaking exams and job interviews: *Have you ever taught children?* — *Yes, I have. I taught at a primary school in 2018.* Experience question → present perfect; the example → past simple.

diagrams.title: Side by side
rows: *Have you ever been to London?* (any time in your life?)
*Did you go to London last summer?* (finished time — fact)
*I've met three British people.* (in my life, so far)
*I met three British people at the conference.* (that finished event)
*She has read a lot of Russian novels.* (ongoing relevance)
*She read War and Peace last winter.* (finished, time stated)

diagrams.text: Czech translates both with the same past tense — English needs the switch.

sections.title: Since & for — present perfect's exclusive territory
sections.content:
Started in the past and **still going on now** → **present perfect** — never present simple. One of the strongest Czech-interference points.
Every interview, CV and "tell me about yourself": *I've worked at TAXVIA for four years. I've been studying English since 2019.* — the most professional-sounding fix for a Czech intermediate speaker.

diagrams.title: Quick rule — both demand present perfect (simple or continuous)

items.label: since + point in time
items.formula: since 2020, since Monday, since I was a child

items.label: for + length of time
items.formula: for three years, for ages, for a long time

items.good: I have lived here for three years.
items.bad: I am living here for three years.

items.good: I have been living here since 2020.
items.why: continuous — often more natural for actions

sections.title: Quick note — simple vs continuous
sections.content:
Present perfect itself splits into **simple** and **continuous** — a second-level decision: get the **tense** right first, refine to continuous to highlight duration or ongoing process.
For past-simple-vs-present-perfect choices, either form usually works. Full treatment: see the separate **Present Perfect (Simple vs Continuous)** topic.

left.title: Present perfect simple
points: I've read that book. (finished — the result)

right.title: Present perfect continuous
points: I've been writing reports all morning.
I've been reading that book. (still going)

sections.title: Common Czech-speaker errors
sections.content:
Five high-frequency mistakes — all driven by Czech having one past tense where English has two. They account for the vast majority of tense errors at B1–B2.
Fix them and you immediately sound more native — especially in interviews, moving between experience (*I have worked, I have studied*) and specific events (*I started there in 2020, I finished my degree last year*).

items.bad: I have been in Prague yesterday.
items.why: *yesterday* = finished time → past simple, never present perfect
*When?* asks for a specific point in time → past simple
started in past + still true now → present perfect (Czech literal: *bydlím tady od 2020*)
*ever / never* about life experience → present perfect
said when the result matters now (why you can't get in) → present perfect

questions.question: Which sentence is correct?
questions.explanation: Yesterday is a finished past time → past simple.

questions.question: We want to ask about life experience (no specific time). Which is natural?

options.a: Did you ever go to Paris?
options.b: Do you ever go to Paris?
options.c: Have you ever been to Paris?

questions.question: She moved here in 2020 and still lives here. Which is correct?
questions.explanation: Since + duration up to now → present perfect.

questions.question: We want to know when something happened. Which is natural?

mc.title: Practice: Past simple vs Present perfect (multiple choice)

options.a: I have been to the cinema yesterday.
options.b: I went to the cinema yesterday.
options.c: I have gone to the cinema yesterday.
options.d: I go to the cinema yesterday.

questions.question: 'She ____ last month.' (She left and is gone.) Which fits?
questions.explanation: Last month = finished time → past simple.

questions.question: 'We ____ here for five years.' (We still live here.) Which fits?
questions.explanation: For + duration up to now → present perfect.

questions.question: Which is wrong?

options.a: When did you start your job?
options.b: Have you ever been to Japan?

questions.explanation: Yesterday → past simple, not present perfect.

questions.question: 'Just' (recently, result now) usually takes:
questions.explanation: Just (recent past, result now) → present perfect.

gapfill.title: Practice: Gap fill (Past simple vs Present perfect)

questions.question: I ____ (see) that film last week.
answers: saw
questions.explanation: Last week = finished past time → past simple.

questions.question: She ____ (live) in London for three years. (She still lives there.)
answers: has lived
questions.explanation: For + duration up to now → present perfect.

questions.question: ____ you ever ____ (be) to Japan?
answers: have you ever been

questions.question: They ____ (go) home an hour ago.
answers: went
questions.explanation: Ago = finished past time → past simple.

questions.question: I ____ (just / finish) my homework.
answers: have just finished
have just finished my homework

questions.question: When ____ you ____ (start) learning English?
answers: did you start

errorcorrection.title: Practice: Error correction (Past simple vs Present perfect)

questions.question: Wrong: 'I have been to the cinema yesterday.' Correct the sentence.
answers: i went to the cinema yesterday
I went to the cinema yesterday.
I went to the cinema yesterday

questions.question: Wrong: 'She has left last month.' Correct the sentence.
answers: she left last month
She left last month.
She left last month

questions.question: Wrong: 'Did you ever try Indian food?' (asking about life experience) Correct the sentence.
answers: have you ever tried indian food
Have you ever tried Indian food?
Have you ever tried Indian food

makesentence.title: Practice: Making sentences (Past simple vs Present perfect)

answers: I saw the film last week.
I saw a film last week.

questions.prompt: she / live / here / five years (still lives here)
answers: She has lived here for five years.
She's lived here for five years.
questions.explanation: Duration up to now → present perfect.

questions.prompt: you / ever / be / New York (form the question)
answers: Have you ever been to New York?
Have you ever been to New York
questions.explanation: Life experience question: Have you ever been to ...? (ever with present perfect).

answers: They have just arrived.
They've just arrived.

makequestion.title: Practice: Making questions (Past simple vs Present perfect)

answers: When did you start your job?
When did you start the job?
Have they ever been to Australia?
Have they ever been to Australia
How long has she had the car?
How long has she had her car?
questions.explanation: How long (duration up to now) → present perfect.
